solution:
Let 2x5+5x4+10x3+10x2+10x+10=fx

Now f-2=-34 and f-1=3

Hence fx has a root in -2,-1

Further f'x=10x4+20x3+30x2+20x+10

=10x2x2+2x+3+2x+1x2

=10x2x2+1x2+2x+1x+3

=10x2x+1x2-2+2x+1x+3

=10x2x+1x+12>0  for all x belongs to R.

fx is strictly increasing function. Since it is an odd degree polynomial it will have exactly one real root.

Hence, fx has only one real root, so a=2.

subject: Mathematics
options: $f^{\prime}$ is not a continuous function of $x$
$f$ is neither increasing nor decreasing function of $x$
solution:
$f(x)=\frac{x}{\sqrt{a^{2}+x^{2}}}-\frac{(d-x)}{\sqrt{b^{2}+(d-x)^{2}}}$

$=\frac{x}{\sqrt{a^{2}+x^{2}}}+\frac{(x-d)}{\sqrt{b^{2}+(x-d)^{2}}}$

$\begin{array}{l}

f^{\prime}(x)=\frac{\sqrt{a^{2}+x^{2}}-\frac{x(2 x)}{2 \sqrt{a^{2}+x^{2}}}}{\left(a^{2}+x^{2}\right)} \\

=\frac{a^{2}+x^{2}-x^{2}}{\left(a^{2}+x^{2}\right)^{3 / 2}}+\frac{b^{2}+(x-d)^{2}-(x-d)^{2}}{\left(b^{2}+(x-d)^{2}\right)^{3 / 2}} \\

+\frac{\sqrt{b^{2}+(x-d)^{2}}-\frac{(x-d) 2(x-d)}{2 \sqrt{b^{2}+(x-d)^{2}}}}{\left(a^{2}+(x-d)^{2}\right)} \\

\Rightarrow f^{\prime}(x)>0, \square x \in R

\end{array}$

$\Rightarrow f(x)$ is increasing function.

Hence, $f(x)$ is increasing function

subject: Mathematics
solution:
Let $y=\cos (x+y)$
$$
\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=-\sin (x+y)\left(1+\frac{d y}{d x}\right)
$$
Now, given equation of tangent is
$$
\begin{aligned}
&x+2 y=k \\
&\Rightarrow \text { Slope }=\frac{-1}{2}
\end{aligned}
$$
So, $\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{-1}{2}$ put this value in (1), we get
$$
\begin{aligned}
&\frac{-1}{2}=-\sin (x+y)\left(1-\frac{1}{2}\right) \\
&\Rightarrow \sin (x+y)=1 \\
&\Rightarrow x+y=\frac{\pi}{2} \Rightarrow y=\frac{\pi}{2}-x
\end{aligned}
$$
Now $\frac{\pi}{2}-x=\cos (x+y)$
$$
\Rightarrow x=\frac{\pi}{2} \text { and } y=0
$$
Thus $x+2 y=k \quad \Rightarrow \frac{\pi}{2}=k$

subject: Mathematics
solution:
If $a$ and $-1$ are the roots of the polynomial, then we get
$$
\begin{aligned}
& f(x)=x^2+(1-a) x-a . \\
\therefore \quad & f(1)=2-2 a \\
\text { and } & f(2)=6-3 a \\
\text { As, } & f(1)+f(2)=0 \\
\Rightarrow & 2-2 a+6-3 a=0 \Rightarrow a=\frac{8}{5}
\end{aligned}
$$
Therefore, the other root is $\frac{8}{5}$

subject: Mathematics
solution:
$$
\begin{aligned}
& f(x)=x e^{x(1-x)}, x \in R \\
& f^{\prime}(x)=e^{x(1-x)} \cdot\left[1+x-2 x^2\right]
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
& =-e^{x(1-x} \cdot\left[2 x^2-x-1\right] \\
& =-2 e^{x(1-x} \cdot\left[\left(x+\frac{1}{2}\right)(x-1]\right) \\
& f^{\prime}(x)=-2 e^{x(1-x)} A \\
& \text { where } A=\left(x+\frac{1}{2}\right)(x-1)
\end{aligned}
$$
Now, exponential function is always $+$ ve and $f^{\prime}(x)$ will be opposite to the sign of $A$
which is -ve in $\left[-\frac{1}{2}, 1\right]$
Hence, $f^{\prime}(x)$ is $+$ ve in $\left[-\frac{1}{2}, 1\right]$
$\therefore f(x)$ is increasing on $\left[-\frac{1}{2}, 1\right]$

subject: Mathematics
solution:
Given $f''(x) > 0$ for all $x \in \mathbb{R}$, which means $f'(x)$ is a strictly increasing function.
We are given $f'(a-1) = 0$. Since $f'(x)$ is strictly increasing, $f'(x) < 0$ for $x < a-1$ and $f'(x) > 0$ for $x > a-1$.
The function $g(x)$ is defined as $g(x) = f(\tan^2 x - 2\tan x + a)$ for $x \in (0, \pi/2)$.
Let $u(x) = \tan^2 x - 2\tan x + a = (\tan x - 1)^2 + a - 1$.
Differentiating $g(x)$ with respect to $x$:
$g'(x) = f'(u(x)) \cdot u'(x) = f'((\tan x - 1)^2 + a - 1) \cdot (2\tan x \sec^2 x - 2\sec^2 x)$
$g'(x) = f'((\tan x - 1)^2 + a - 1) \cdot 2\sec^2 x (\tan x - 1)$.

Case 1: $x \in (0, \pi/4)$.
In this interval, $0 < \tan x < 1$, so $(\tan x - 1) < 0$.
Also, $(\tan x - 1)^2 > 0$, so $u(x) = (\tan x - 1)^2 + a - 1 > a - 1$.
Since $f'(x)$ is increasing and $f'(a-1) = 0$, for $u(x) > a - 1$, we have $f'(u(x)) > 0$.
Thus, $g'(x) = (+)(+)(-) < 0$. So $g(x)$ is decreasing in $(0, \pi/4)$.
Statement (I) is False.

Case 2: $x \in (\pi/4, \pi/2)$.
In this interval, $\tan x > 1$, so $(\tan x - 1) > 0$.
Also, $(\tan x - 1)^2 > 0$, so $u(x) = (\tan x - 1)^2 + a - 1 > a - 1$.
Thus, $f'(u(x)) > 0$.
Then $g'(x) = (+)(+)(+) > 0$. So $g(x)$ is increasing in $(\pi/4, \pi/2)$.
Statement (II) is False.

Therefore, neither (I) nor (II) is true.

solution:
Given equations are x2a2+y2 b2=1 ...1 and x2+y2=ab.....2 where  a>b.

From 1,

b2x2+a2y2=a2b2

Substituting value of y2 from 2 in the above equation

b2x2+a2ab-x2=a2b2

⇒b2-a2x2=a2b2-a3b
⇒x2=ba2b-ab2-a2=a2ba+b

∴x=a2ba+b

Substituting value of x2 in 2, we get

y2=ab2a+b

∴y=ab2a+b
Point of intersection is a2ba+b, ab2a+b

Now, from 1,
x2a2+y2b2=1

⇒2xa2+2yb2dydx=0
⇒dydx=m1=-b2xa2y

From 2,
x2+y2=ab

⇒2x+2y·dydx=0

⇒dydx=m2=-xy

Let θ be the angle between curve 1 & 2.
∴tanθ=m1-m21+m1m2=-b2xa2y+xy1+b2x2a2y2=xya2-b2b2x2+a2y2=xya2-b2a2b2

∴tanθa2ba+b, ab2a+b=a3b3(a+b)2·a2-b2a2b2=(a-b)ab

Hence, θ=tan-1a-bab

The quadratic curve passing through the point -1,0 and touching the line y=x at 1,1 be y=fx,

⇒a-b+c=0  ....i

Passes through 1, 1

Now given tangent to y=x at 1,1,

So, dydx=1

⇒  2a+b = 1 ....…iii

Solving all above three equations we get,

a=14, b=12 and c=14

Hence, the equation of curve is y=x24+x2+14

Now α,α+1 lies on curve

α+1=α24+α2+14

⇒(α-3)(α+1)=0

⇒α=3
Point in first quadrant is 3,4

Now finding slope of tangent at 3,4 we get,

dydx3,4=x2+123,4=2

⇒ Slope of normal =-12

Hence, equation of normal will be, y-4=-12(x-3)

So, x- intercept = 11

subject: Mathematics
solution:
Sphere of radius $r=3 \mathrm{~cm}$ Let $b, h$ be base radius and height of cone respectively. So, volume of cone $=\frac{1}{2} \pi b^2 h$
In right angled $\triangle A B C$ by Pythagoras theorem $$ \begin{aligned} &(h-r)^2+b^2=r^2 \\ \Rightarrow & b^2=r^2-(h-r)^2=r^2-\left(h^2-2 h r+\mathrm{r}^2\right)=2 h r-h^2 \\ \therefore & \text { Volume }(v) \frac{1}{3} \pi h\left[2 h r-h^2\right]=\frac{1}{3}\left[2 h r^2-h^3\right] \\ & \frac{d v}{d h}=\frac{1}{3}\left[4 h r-3 h^2\right]=0 \Rightarrow h(4 r-3 h)=0 \\ & \frac{d^2 v}{d h^2}=\frac{1}{3}[4 r-6 h] \end{aligned} $$ At $h=\frac{4 r}{3}, \frac{d^2 v}{d h^2}=\frac{1}{3}\left[4 r-\frac{4 r}{3} \times 6\right]=\frac{1}{3}[4 r-8 r < ] 0$ $\Rightarrow$ maximum volume ocurs at $h$ $$ =\frac{4 r}{3}=\frac{4}{3} \times 3=4 \mathrm{~cm} $$ As from (1), $$ (h-r)^2+b^2=r^2 $$ $$ \Rightarrow b^2=2 h r-h^2=2 \cdot \frac{4 r}{3} r-\frac{16 r^2}{9} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} &=\frac{8 r^2}{3}-\frac{16 r^2}{9} \\ &=\frac{(24-16) r^2}{9}=\frac{8 r^2}{9} \\ &\Rightarrow b=\frac{2 \sqrt{2}}{3} r=2 \sqrt{2} \mathrm{~cm} \end{aligned} $$ Therefore curved surface area $$ \begin{aligned} &=\pi b l=\pi b \sqrt{h^2+r^2} \\ &=\pi 2 \sqrt{2} \sqrt{4^2+8}=8 \sqrt{3} \pi \mathrm{cm}^2 \end{aligned} $$

subject: Mathematics
solution:
$\begin{aligned} & \left(3 f^{\prime} f^{\prime \prime}+f f^{\prime \prime \prime}\right)(x)=\left(\left(f f^{\prime \prime}+\left(f^{\prime}\right)^2\right)(x)\right)^{\prime} \\ & \left(f f f^{\prime \prime}+\left(f^{\prime}\right)^2\right)(x)=\left(\left(f f^{\prime}\right)(x)\right)^{\prime} \\ & \therefore\left(3 f^{\prime} f^{\prime \prime}+f^{\prime \prime \prime}\right)(x)=\left(f(x) \cdot f^{\prime}(x)\right)^{\prime \prime}\end{aligned}$
$\begin{aligned} & \min \text { roots of } \mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x}) \rightarrow 4 \\ & \therefore \min \text {. roots of } \mathrm{f}^{\prime}(\mathrm{x}) \rightarrow 3 \\ & \therefore \min \text { roots of }\left(\mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x}) \cdot \mathrm{f}^{\prime}(\mathrm{x})\right) \rightarrow 7 \\ & \therefore \min \text { roots of }\left(\mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x}) \cdot \mathrm{f}^{\prime}(\mathrm{x})\right)^{\prime \prime} \rightarrow 5\end{aligned}$
